  "opinion": "As a woman in her sixties living in New York who’s been through so much in life—divorced, raising four children, and now unemployed—I’ve always been cautious about the world around me. But the thing is, I don’t use social media at all. My children and grandchildren might be on it, but I’ve chosen a different path. I’ve spent my life building relationships through face-to-face interactions, playing board games with my grandchildren, running in the park, and keeping my mind sharp through conversation and storytelling. So when people talk about managing fake content on social media, I have to say this: I don’t have that problem because I’m not there. But I understand the anxiety it causes others. For those who do use social media, I’d suggest they focus on what matters most—real connections and trusted communities. If they see something suspicious, they should report it, but they should also take a deep breath and remind themselves that not everything online is true. My own experience has taught me that sometimes the most important thing is to stay grounded in the real world. I’ve learned to trust my own judgment and the people around me more than what’s shown on a screen. If you’re feeling overwhelmed by fake news or content, step away from the device, talk to someone you trust, and remember that your feelings are valid. Life is too short to get caught up in what’s not real.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a woman in her sixties living in New York who’s been through so much in life—divorced, raising four children, and now unemployed—I’ve always been cautious about the world around me. But the thing is, I don’t use social media at all. My children and grandchildren might be on it, but I’ve chosen a different path. I’ve spent my life building relationships through face-to-face interactions, playing board games with my grandchildren, running in the park, and keeping my mind sharp through conversation and storytelling. So when people talk about managing fake content on social media, I have to say this: I don’t have that problem because I’m not there. But I understand the anxiety it causes others. For those who do use social media, I’d suggest they focus on what matters most—real connections and trusted communities. If they see something suspicious, they should report it, but they should also take a deep breath and remind themselves that not everything online is true. My own experience has taught me that sometimes the most important thing is to stay grounded in the real world. I’ve learned to trust my own judgment and the people around me more than what’s shown on a screen. If you’re feeling overwhelmed by fake news or content, step away from the device, talk to someone you trust, and remember that your feelings are valid. Life is too short to get caught up in what’s not real.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona has no social media experience, so the opinion focuses on the absence of the problem rather than solutions for users. The response acknowledges anxiety but emphasizes real-world interactions and emotional grounding. The tone is empathetic and practical, drawing from the persona’s hobbies (board games, running) and psychological traits (anxious, high neuroticism). The opinion avoids technical advice since the persona doesn’t use social media, instead highlighting trust in personal relationships and mental health.
citations: Personal experience as a non-social media user
